title: Free workshop invite for paid subscribers
questions: How can leaders communicate intent, progress, investment, and ROI more effectively?
What makes a workshop format feel fulfilling and broadly useful beyond a single product?
How should you structure and invite participants for early-stage workshop iteration?
summary: The post invites paid subscribers to a small, free workshop being developed for leaders who want to communicate intent, progress, investment, and ROI more intentionally. It draws on the idea of “north star” workshops that are tangential to a product so participants can learn without needing the product itself. The invitation emphasizes focused, non-broadcast participation to help refine the workshop format and content.

title: TBM 349: A "Product Transformation" North Star
questions: What percentage of product teams in my company can operate fairly independently with clear line of impact to customers and the business?
How can we tell whether teams are making real progress with actionable leading indicators rather than vague milestones?
What organizational structures prevent teams from moving toward a vision of how work should look?
How should we measure product transformation without causing blame games or defensive reactions?
search_terms: leading indicators for product team success
north star metric naming and blame avoidance
think big work small strategy execution
funding model for 0–1 vs growth margins
product team operating model health metric
summary: The post proposes a “product transformation” north star defined as the percentage of product teams that can operate independently with clear customer impact and demonstrate progress via actionable leading indicators. It argues that many companies get stuck around 50–60% due to structural constraints and legacy inertia, leading to “institutionalized incoherence,” and that measurement must be designed to avoid blame and defensiveness. It’s aimed at product leaders and transformation practitioners who want a practical way to assess and improve how teams work as they scale.

title: North Star Framework Template & Activity Library
questions: Where can I find reusable North Star Framework templates and workshop activities?
What materials come with a North Star workshop template library (videos, articles, playbook links)?
How do I access and use the North Star templates in Miro?
search_terms: north star metric workshop miro board
summary: This post shares a reusable Miro-based template and activity library for running North Star Framework workshops. It’s aimed at product teams and facilitators who want ready-to-use workshop assets, with guidance materials like videos and curated references. Readers can copy the templates (with a free Miro account) and use the board as a collaborative resource.

title: TBM 45/52: Big Disrupted Enterprises vs.  "The Best"
questions: Why is organizational transformation so much harder in disrupted enterprises than in digital-product-first companies?
Which practices from Silicon Valley (OKRs, empowered teams) actually transfer to large enterprises, and which do not?
Are digital-product-first “best” companies healthy and psychologically safe places to work, or is the pedestal partly luck and money?
How should leaders design change efforts when investor patience is limited and inertia is strong?
search_terms: north star inputs and flywheels deep dive
organizational inertia and status quo bias
business agility antipatterns sooner safer happier
summary: The post contrasts rapidly growing digital-product-first companies with large disrupted enterprises, arguing that transformation difficulty and constraints differ dramatically. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners designing change, especially those tempted to copy BigTech playbooks like OKRs and empowered teams. The author calls for more empathy and contextual awareness when evaluating “the best” and “lumbering” organizations.

title: TBM 35/52: Vanity Metric vs. Meaningful Metric Activity
questions: How do we distinguish vanity metrics from meaningful metrics for our North Star?
What North Star Metric name would feel inspiring and human-centric to our team?
How can we avoid analysis paralysis when defining measurement for a North Star Metric?
What is the fastest way to align a team on OKRs and a North Star Framework?
search_terms: north star metric vanity vs meaningful
how to name a north star metric
summary: The post offers a practical facilitation shortcut for teams defining a North Star Metric within an OKRs/North Star Framework context. It contrasts vanity metrics with meaningful, strategy-validating metrics and uses a human-centric visualization exercise to help teams overcome measurement analysis paralysis. It’s aimed at product and analytics leaders who need a fast way to align on a compelling metric and start measuring.

title: TBM 28/53: Words Before Numbers (North Star Statement Exercise)
questions: How do we clarify our own strategy and assumptions before defining metrics?
What prompts or steps help teams refine a North Star statement quickly?
Should we define measurement before or after we align on a strategy narrative?
How can we choose metrics that support learning and reduce uncertainty?
summary: The post shares a workshop exercise for product teams using the North Star Framework that emphasizes clarifying words, assumptions, and strategy before selecting metrics. It’s aimed at teams that tend to jump straight to measurement or copy other companies, and it guides them to refine a short North Star statement and only then consider metrics that enable learning and reduce uncertainty.

title: TBM 6/53: Way and Why
questions: How do I introduce a framework like the North Star Framework in my organization without becoming “the framework person”?
Why do people misread my intent when I push a process or method?
What does it mean to lead with the why instead of the way in team communication?
How can I test whether my teammates share the underlying problem or opportunity before proposing a solution?
search_terms: leading with the why vs the way
how to introduce OKRs or north star framework
avoid being pigeonholed as the process person
north star framework autonomy with alignment
how to sell a framework to a team
experiment with distancing from the solution
summary: The post offers guidance for leaders and practitioners on how to introduce popular frameworks (using the North Star Framework as an example) without being perceived as pushing process for its own sake. It argues that adoption succeeds more often when you lead with the underlying why—an opportunity, problem, or observation—using specific, first-person language to reduce intent/perception gaps. It’s aimed at people trying to influence teams toward better ways of working while maintaining trust and shared ownership.
